title: Bacterial Diarrhea -- Treatment / Management
content: Most cases of acute diarrhea are self-limited and only require supportive care. Rehydration is the mainstay of treatment, preferably orally with a solution containing water, sugar, and salt. Commercially available or WHO-provided oral rehydration solutions are acceptable. Children can begin with 5 mL every 1 to 2 minutes to avoid too much liquid in the stomach at one time. Clinicians reserve IVF replacement for patients who cannot tolerate oral fluids or are moderately to severely dehydrated. The rate of fluid replacement depends on the level of volume depletion.

D: 5% glucose solution

title: Adult Dehydration -- Treatment / Management -- Treat Underlying Causes
content: Effective management of dehydration involves replacing fluid and electrolytes and addressing the underlying causes. Treating the root cause helps prevent recurrence and promotes a more complete and lasting recovery. Diarrhea or vomiting: Antiemetics, such as ondansetron or metoclopramide, and antidiarrheals, such as loperamide for noninfectious diarrhea, are recommended. Diabetes-related dehydration: In DKA, hyperglycemia should be managed with insulin, and fluid replacement should be provided in cases of hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (HHS). Sepsis-induced dehydration: Early IV fluid resuscitation should be initiated, along with prompt antibiotic therapy. Diuretic-induced dehydration: Medication should be adjusted, and lost electrolytes should be replenished to restore fluid and electrolyte balance.

title: Cholera(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
content: The mainstay of treatment of cholera is prompt fluid resuscitation based on the degree of volume depletion. If an estimated 5% to 10% of body weight has been lost, oral rehydration solution should be used. Clinical trials have shown that rice-based oral rehydration solution can shorten the duration of diarrhea and the amount of stool loss. In an emergency, a solution can be made, consisting of one liter of water, mixed with six teaspoons of sugar and a half teaspoon of salt. For patients in hypovolemic shock or greater than 10% loss of body weight, intravenous fluids should be administered. Approximately 100 mL/kg of lactated ringers should be administered during the first three hours. Prompt treatment of severe cholera with fluids can reduce the mortality from over 10% to less than 0.5%. [7] [10] [11]

title: Adult Dehydration -- Treatment / Management -- Severe Dehydration
content: When a patient presents with severe dehydration or cannot tolerate ORT, IV fluid therapy is necessary to rapidly restore fluid balance and correct electrolyte disturbances. Common IV fluids used include: Isotonic crystalloids (first-line treatment): Normal saline (0.9%) is preferred for treating hypovolemia and most dehydration cases. While effective, administering large volumes may lead to hyperchloremic acidosis. [27] Lactated ringers contain bicarbonate precursors, which are beneficial in acidotic states, such as in patients with diarrhea or sepsis. Hypotonic fluids (for hypernatremic dehydration): Saline (half normal saline, 0.45%) is used to gradually correct hypernatremia while reducing the risk of cerebral edema. Dextrose-containing fluids (for hypoglycemia or ongoing fluid loss): 5% Dextrose with 0.9% normal saline (D5-0.9% normal saline) is administered with saline to prevent dilutional hyponatremia, which could occur with 5% dextrose in water (D5W) infusion alone.

content: The treatment of choice is a low-osmolarity fluid, with 75 mEq of Na, 64 mEq of Cl, 20 mEq of K, and 75 mmol of glucose per liter; the total osmolarity should be 245 mOsm/L. In mild to moderate emesis, give ondansetron (4 mg for children 4 to 11 years, and double the doses for older children) is often considered for better oral rehydration. IV rehydration is done with a lactated Ringer's solution or normal saline at 20 mL/Kg body weight.

title: Travelers Diarrhea -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Outcomes
content: The prognosis for most patients with traveler's diarrhea is excellent. However, thousands of patients go to the emergency departments each year looking for a magical cure. Hydration is the key and admission is only required for severe dehydration and orthostatic hypotension. title: Travelers Diarrhea -- Treatment / Management
content: The foundation of diarrhea management is fluid repletion. In mild cases, travelers should focus on increasing water intake. Water is usually sufficient though sports drinks and other electrolyte fluids can be used. Pedialyte can be used for pediatric patients. Milk and juices should be avoided as this can worsen diarrhea. In more severe cases, oral rehydration salt can be used to ensure rehydration with adequate electrolyte repletion. In cases of severe dehydration, IV fluids may ultimately be required.

title: Pediatric Dehydration -- Treatment / Management -- Mild to Moderate Dehydration
content: The WHO recommends ORT solutions with a total osmolarity of 200 to 310 mOsm/L, glucose content of less than 20 g/L, sodium content of 60 to 90 mEq/L, and potassium content of 15 to 25 mEq/L. This balanced mixture of electrolyte salts and glucose is available as a pharmaceutical product distributed in packets. In many countries, other oral rehydration solutions (ORS) with similar concentrations are available in powdered, liquid, or "ice-pop" forms, often sweetened with sucralose for increased palatability. Fluids with high sugar content, including juice, soda, sweet tea, and sports drinks, might worsen diarrhea due to the unabsorbable glucose load and decreased water absorption by the intestinal lumen. Broths with excessive salt can cause hypernatremia. The WHO and AAP recommend standard, prepared ORS to avoid complications from drinking homemade preparations and everyday beverages. [13] ORT is contraindicated in patients with altered mental status and at risk for aspiration or those with abdominal ileus, malabsorption, persistent vomiting, or severe dehydration requiring higher fluid volumes.

title: Pediatric Dehydration -- Treatment / Management -- Severe Dehydration
content: Intravenous glucose, 0.5 to 1 g/kg, can correct hypoglycemia. A practical guideline is administering 5 to 10 mL/kg of a 10% dextrose solution (D10) or 2 to 4 mL/kg of a 25% dextrose solution (D25). Adolescents with a large bore intravenous line in place can receive 1 to 2 mL/kg of a 50% dextrose solution (D50). [16] 0.9% saline is the preferred initial resuscitation fluid for volume replacement. Alternatives include balanced isotonic solutions such as lactated Ringer solution, which is not clinically superior to normal saline. [17] Dextrose is generally not recommended for the replacement phase of fluid administration unless documented hypoglycemia exists.

title: Viral Diarrhea -- Treatment / Management
content: The primary concerns in viral gastroenteritis are fluid and electrolyte imbalances. Sports drinks and broths can support adults with acute viral gastroenteritis without signs of volume depletion. Adults exhibiting mild-to-moderate volume depletion should receive treatment with oral rehydration solutions, which are preferable to sports drinks for maintaining proper electrolyte balance and hydration. Individuals experiencing severe hypovolemia or those unable to tolerate oral rehydration necessitate intravenous fluid repletion. Patients should be encouraged to consume food as their tolerance allows without imposing strict limitations. Recommendations may include choosing smaller meals and incorporating bland or low-residue food options in their diet. [16] [17]

content: There are many causes of diarrhea, and the condition is best managed by an interprofessional team that includes nurses and pharmacists. Most cases of diarrhea can be prevented by maintaining good personal hygiene and handwashing. In addition, the key is to hydrate the patients. The outcomes for well-hydrated patients are excellent, but patients at extremes of age may not tolerate any degree of dehydration. [31] [32]

title: Factitious Disorder -- Evaluation
content: Absence of supporting lab work, imaging, or other diagnostic findings for the presumed disease. Specific labwork proving the falsification of symptoms. For example, in patients with factitious diarrhea, a stool sample may be positive for laxatives, or stool osmolality may be consistent with water. In non-diabetic hypoglycemia, testing may be done to rule out exogenous insulin use. The patient is witnessed inducing symptoms or interfering with treatment. A search of the patient’s room reveals a means of inducing symptoms. (eg, syringes, laxatives, blood pressure medications, sedatives, illicit drugs) This is often part of the consent form signed by a patient upon admission. The patient seems overly eager to undergo extensive workup or treatment. The patient predicts or demonstrates a decline when faced with discharge from the hospital. The patient admits to the behavior when confronted. [3] [4]

Clinicians should calculate fluid requirements for children based on weight. title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2157
content: Increased plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels (hyperglycemia). This rise in plasma osmolality often originates from dehydration, as fluid loss concentrates solutes in the bloodstream. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume (hypovolemia). Recognizing that dehydration can lead to higher plasma osmolality, which in turn may signal hyperglycemia and accompany hypovolemia, is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate fluid and glucose management in patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2237
content: Blood substitutes and perfusion solutions constitute a specific category of intravenous (IV) solutions that are administered parenterally to deliver fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients. As a subclass of IV solutions used for parenteral administration, these products are formulated to replace or augment blood components, provide rapid volume expansion, and supply essential nutrients when oral intake is not possible. They play a critical role in clinical settings such as fluid resuscitation for hypovolemia, management of blood loss, and provision of nutritional support for patients who require intravenous therapy.

title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 446
content: CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROLYTES is classified as a member of Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance, which are a subset of iv solutions used in parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes and nutrients. These intravenous solutions are designed to modify electrolyte concentrations during fluid therapy and may also include Solutions producing osmotic diuresis, another subclass of iv solutions used for parenteral delivery of fluids, electrolytes and nutrients that promote water excretion through osmotic mechanisms. In addition, CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROLYTES has a defined relationship with the group ELECTROLYTES WITH CARBOHYDRATES, ANTIDIARRHEALS, INTESTINAL ANTIINFLAMMATORY/ANTIINFECTIVE AGENTS, indicating that formulations combining electrolytes and carbohydrates often incorporate antidiarrheal agents and intestinal anti‑inflammatory or anti‑infective medications. Together, these relationships illustrate how carbohydrate‑electrolyte preparations fit within broader categories of intravenous fluid therapy aimed at correcting electrolyte imbalances, providing osmotic diuresis when needed, and delivering adjunctive gastrointestinal agents.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 782
content: Blood and related products include a subcategory called blood substitutes and perfusion solutions. Blood substitutes and perfusion solutions, in turn, encompass IV solutions used in parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes and nutrients. Within this group of IV solutions, there are specific formulations identified as solutions producing osmotic diuresis. Thus, solutions producing osmotic diuresis are a specialized type of IV solution used for parenteral delivery of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients, and these IV solutions are considered part of the broader class of blood substitutes and perfusion solutions that belong to the overall group of blood and related products. This hierarchical organization helps clinicians understand how various fluid therapies relate to one another and supports appropriate selection of products for volume replacement, electrolyte management, or targeted diuretic effect in patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 158
content: Blood Substitutes and Perfusion Solutions represent a broad therapeutic class that encompasses irrigating solutions, blood substitutes, perfusion agents, and intravenous solutions used for parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients, as well as blood and related products. Within the irrigating‑solutions subgroup, the categories “Other irrigating solutions in ATC” and “Salt irrigating solutions” are each classified under the group “irrigating solutions, blood substitutes and perfusion.” “Other irrigating solutions in ATC” is associated with glycin, whereas “Salt irrigating solutions” is associated with hydrogenuhličitan sodný (sodium bicarbonate). Carbohydrate/Electrolytes belong to the class “Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance,” which itself is a subclass of the intravenous solutions used for parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients. These carbohydrate/electrolyte preparations are linked to formulations that combine electrolytes with carbohydrates, antidiarrheals, and intestinal anti‑inflammatory/anti‑infective agents. Both “Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance” and “Solutions producing osmotic diuresis” are types of intravenous solutions used for parenteral fluid, electrolyte, and nutrient delivery. This hierarchical organization clarifies how various fluid, electrolyte, and nutrient delivery systems—ranging from irrigating and perfusion solutions to specialized carbohydrate/electrolyte and osmotic‑diuretic formulations—are interrelated, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate products for irrigation, perfusion, electrolyte management, and blood‑product replacement.

title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 122
content: Blood substitutes and perfusion solutions comprise a broad therapeutic class that includes both irrigating solutions and intravenous (IV) solutions used for parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, nutrients, and plasma‑derived or synthetic colloids. Within this hierarchy, the subcategory “irrigating solutions, blood substitutes and perfusion” contains other irrigating solutions (e.g., glycin) and salt irrigating solutions (e.g., sodium bicarbonate). The IV‑solution branch contains formulations that affect electrolyte balance; the carbohydrate/electrolyte group links agents that combine electrolytes with carbohydrates, antidiarrheals, and intestinal anti‑inflammatory/anti‑infective drugs. The “blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions” subgroup includes O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin, dextran, and hydroxyethyl‑starch. title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 936
content: Electrolyte solutions (I.V. additive) comprise several specific compounds that are used to provide essential electrolytes during intravenous therapy. Magnesium phosphate is included as a source of magnesium, while octan draselný (potassium acetate) supplies potassium. Sodium glycerophosphate contributes sodium and phosphate, and octan sodný (sodium acetate) provides additional sodium. Together, these agents are classified under the category of electrolyte solutions for intravenous administration, ensuring balanced electrolyte replacement and supporting metabolic needs in patients receiving IV fluids.

content: Bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate are both used therapeutically to manage diarrhea (الإِسْهال) and broader gastrointestinal disturbances (اضْطِراب مَعِدِيّ). In contrast, magnesium hydroxide (hydroxid hořečnatý) can provoke diarrhea as an adverse effect. Thus, while magnesium hydroxide may induce diarrheal symptoms, the bismuth compounds serve as treatments for those same symptoms and for related gastrointestinal disorders, highlighting their complementary roles in patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 5 -- Community 2962
content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 2) and with reduced blood volume (قلة حجم الدم). Type 2 diabetes can predispose patients to acute kidney injury through chronic hyperglycemia‑induced vascular and tubular damage, making the kidneys more vulnerable to hemodynamic stress. Conversely, hypovolemia decreases renal perfusion and can directly precipitate acute kidney failure. Recognizing that both type 2 diabetes and low intravascular volume are important clinical contributors to acute kidney failure guides clinicians to monitor diabetic patients closely, ensure adequate hydration, and intervene promptly to preserve renal function.

title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1884
content: Magnesium citrate is an osmotic laxative that may be used to treat إِمْساك (constipation). However, its administration is contraindicated in patients with ثقب معوي (intestinal perforation), as the drug can worsen this condition. Therefore, clinicians should consider magnesium citrate for constipation only after confirming the absence of an intestinal perforation.
